answer:
The shared rule examined here is the inheritance right of the kshetraja (field-born) son when an aurasa (own-born, legitimate) son also exists.

Manu's rule (Manusmriti, chapter 9, Mn_9.162–9.165): Manu acknowledges both the aurasa and kshetraja as co-heirs. The aurasa is the primary master of the estate, but he is required by Dharma to give the kshetraja a sixth or fifth part of the paternal wealth when dividing (Mn_9.164). Both sons are thus statutory co-inheritors of the paternal estate, even when the aurasa is present.

Yajnavalkya's rule (Yajnavalkya Smriti, chapter 2, Yj_2.128 and Yj_2.132): Yajnavalkya ranks the twelve son-types in strict hierarchy: aurasa first, putrikasuta (son of an appointed daughter) second, kshetraja third. The governing rule is Yj_2.132: 'Among these, in the absence of a preceding one, each succeeding one is the giver of the funeral offering and the taker of a share.' This means the kshetraja only takes a share when there is no aurasa (and no putrikasuta). If an aurasa exists, the kshetraja inherits nothing.

Verdict: Yajnavalkya tightens Manu. Manu grants the kshetraja a mandatory fractional share (1/6 to 1/5 of the estate) even when an aurasa exists alongside him. Yajnavalkya's strict hierarchical principle cuts the kshetraja out entirely the moment an aurasa is present — a direct restriction of the partial co-inheritance Manu had provided.
